items.q: Why can a cold winter NOT disprove climate change?
items.correct: Climate is the long-term statistical average; a single cold winter is a weather event. Climate change is the trend in averages and extremes over decades, not the absence of cold days.
items.misconception: A really cold winter proves global warming is not happening.
items.random_wrong: Climate change only happens in summer.
items.why: This is one of the most common public misconceptions. Climate is the slope of the trend over 30+ years; individual weather events are scatter around that line — both can be cold.

items.q: What weather does an anticyclone (high pressure system) bring?
items.correct: Descending air warms and dries, suppressing cloud formation — so anticyclones bring clear, settled weather: hot in summer, cold and frosty in winter.
items.misconception: High pressure brings stormy weather because the air pushes down hard.
items.random_wrong: Anticyclones bring heavy rainfall because they trap moisture.
items.why: Students conflate 'high pressure' with 'strong weather'. In fact, sinking air evaporates clouds — depressions (low pressure with rising air) bring storms, not anticyclones.

items.q: What is the difference between the tundra and the taiga biomes?
items.correct: Tundra is treeless cold biome (too cold and permafrost-dominated for trees), while taiga (boreal forest) is the band of coniferous forest just south of the tundra in subarctic latitudes.
items.misconception: Tundra and taiga are the same biome with different names.
items.random_wrong: Tundra is in the Arctic and taiga is in the Antarctic.
items.why: There is no taiga in the Antarctic because there is no continental land at that latitude. The biomes are distinct (treeless vs forested) — defined by whether the growing season permits trees.

items.q: Why are the world's great hot deserts found around 30°N and 30°S?
items.correct: Sinking dry air from high pressure belts
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Sinking dry air from high pressure belts. “Tectonic plate boundaries” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which biome is characterised by PERMAFROST?
items.why: Correct answer: Tundra. “Tropical rainforest” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Approximately what maximum annual rainfall (mm) defines a hot desert?
items.why: Exact answer: 250. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
